---
title: "Graphs and Trees"
description: "How vertices, edges, paths, cycles, connectedness, and trees organize finite relational structure."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: discrete-math
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/discrete-math/concepts/counting-and-combinatorics.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/logic/concepts/sets-functions-and-relations.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Read a graph as a finite relational object built from vertices and edges."
  - "Distinguish paths, cycles, connectedness, and trees."
  - "Use degree counting and the handshaking lemma correctly."
  - "Recognize trees as the simplest connected acyclic graphs and as load-bearing objects for algorithms and structure."
applications:
  - algorithms
  - graph-theory
  - networks
keywords:
  - graph
  - tree
  - path
  - cycle
  - connectedness
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/foundation/discrete-math/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/discrete-math/concepts/counting-and-combinatorics.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/discrete-math/concepts/recurrences-and-asymptotics.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/logic/concepts/sets-functions-and-relations.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/index.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT Mathematics for Computer Science"
  - "Stanford CS103"
  - "CMU 15-151"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 6.1200J graph theory lecture"
  - "MIT 6.1200J Mathematics for Computer Science"
  - "MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science"
  - "Stanford CS103 graphs lecture I"
  - "Stanford CS103 graphs lecture II"
  - "CMU 15-151 course page"
---

## Role

This page is the third step of the discrete-math module.

It shifts the module from counting and recurrence patterns into relational structure:

- objects as vertices
- relationships as edges
- movement as paths
- feedback as cycles
- minimal connected structure as trees

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Recurrences and Asymptotics](recurrences-and-asymptotics.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- what a graph is
- what paths, cycles, and connectedness mean
- what the degree of a vertex measures
- why the handshaking lemma is true
- why trees are the simplest connected acyclic graphs

## Why It Matters

Graphs are one of the first places where discrete math starts to model structure directly instead of only counting possibilities.

They show up whenever we care about:

- communication links
- dependency structure
- routing and search
- social or biological networks
- state transitions

Trees matter even more than they first appear to:

- recursive definitions often induce trees
- search procedures often build trees
- hierarchical data is tree-shaped
- spanning trees are the first simplification of a network that still preserves connectivity

## Prerequisite Recall

- [Counting and Combinatorics](counting-and-combinatorics.qmd) already built the habit of describing finite objects precisely
- [Sets, Functions, Relations](../../logic/concepts/sets-functions-and-relations.qmd) already treated structure through membership and pairing
- graph arguments often use simple case reasoning, structural definitions, and counting together

## Intuition

A graph is a finite collection of objects together with a rule saying which pairs are linked.

So a graph lets us ask structural questions like:

- can one point reach another?
- is there a loop?
- how many links touch each point?
- can the whole network be simplified while keeping connectivity?

This makes graphs different from pure counting.

Counting asks `how many`.

Graph theory often asks `how are things connected`.

Trees are the cleanest answer to that second question. They are connected enough that every vertex can reach every other, but sparse enough that no cycles remain.

## Formal Core

::: {#def-graph}
## Definition: Graph

A simple undirected graph consists of:

- a finite set of vertices $V$
- a set of edges $E$, where each edge is an unordered pair $\{u,v\}$ of distinct vertices

We often write

$$
G = (V,E).
$$

The graph records which vertices are adjacent.
:::

::: {#def-path-cycle}
## Definition: Path and Cycle

A path is a sequence of vertices

$$
v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k
$$

such that each consecutive pair is joined by an edge.

A cycle is a closed path of positive length that returns to its starting vertex without repeating intermediate vertices.

Paths encode reachability. Cycles encode structural feedback or redundancy.
:::

::: {#def-degree-connected-tree}
## Definition: Degree, Connectedness, Tree

The degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it.

A graph is connected if every pair of vertices can be joined by a path.

A tree is a connected graph with no cycles.
:::

::: {#thm-handshaking}
## Theorem Idea: Handshaking Lemma

For any finite undirected graph,

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2|E|.
$$

Each edge contributes exactly `1` to the degree count of each of its two endpoints, so every edge is counted twice.
:::

::: {#thm-tree-characterization}
## Theorem Idea: Tree Characterization

If a graph with $n$ vertices is connected and has exactly

$$
n-1
$$

edges, then it is a tree.

Equivalently, a tree on $n$ vertices always has exactly $n-1$ edges.

This is one of the first clean examples where connectedness and sparsity balance perfectly.
:::

::: {#thm-spanning-tree}
## Theorem Idea: Spanning Tree

Every connected graph contains a spanning tree:

- it keeps all the vertices
- it stays connected
- it removes enough edges to eliminate cycles

So trees are the minimal connected skeleton inside a connected graph.
:::

## Worked Example

Consider the graph with

$$
V = \{A,B,C,D,E\}
$$

and edges

$$
E = \{\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{C,D\}, \{C,E\}\}.
$$

### Step 1: Compute degrees

- $\deg(A)=2$
- $\deg(B)=1$
- $\deg(C)=3$
- $\deg(D)=1$
- $\deg(E)=1$

So

$$
2 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 1 = 8.
$$

There are $4$ edges, so the handshaking lemma predicts

$$
2|E| = 2 \cdot 4 = 8,
$$

which matches.

### Step 2: Check connectedness

Every vertex can be reached from $C$, and therefore every pair of vertices is joined by some path.

So the graph is connected.

### Step 3: Check whether it has a cycle

There is no closed loop:

- $B$ only touches $A$
- $D$ only touches $C$
- $E$ only touches $C$

So no cycle can form.

### Step 4: Recognize the tree structure

The graph has:

- $5$ vertices
- $4$ edges

Since it is connected and has $5-1=4$ edges, it is a tree.

This example matters because it shows the standard first-pass workflow:

- count degrees
- test connectivity
- test whether cycles remain
- use the `n-1` edge rule as a clean structural check

## Computation Lens

For first-pass graph work, the main habits are:

1. write down the vertex set and edge set explicitly
2. compute degrees
3. ask whether paths connect all vertices
4. ask whether a cycle appears
5. if the graph is connected, look for a spanning tree or check whether it already is one

Many algorithmic graph procedures are just larger versions of those same habits:

- search for reachability
- remove or detect cycles
- preserve connectivity with fewer edges

## Application Lens

Graphs and trees are the discrete language of many later subjects.

They appear in:

- search and shortest-path algorithms
- communication networks
- dependency graphs
- spanning-tree and routing problems
- message passing and graph learning
- control graphs and state transitions

Trees are especially important because they are the first sparse structure that is still fully connected.

That makes them the cleanest place to study recursion, traversal, and hierarchical organization.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- what a graph is
- what paths, cycles, and connectedness mean
- why the sum of all degrees is twice the number of edges
- why a connected graph with $n-1$ edges is a tree

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

The next pages in this module are:

- [Discrete Probability Bridge](discrete-probability-bridge.qmd)
- [Number Theory Basics](number-theory-basics.qmd)

The strongest adjacent bridges are:

- [Discrete Probability Bridge](discrete-probability-bridge.qmd)
- [Counting and Combinatorics](counting-and-combinatorics.qmd)
- [Recurrences and Asymptotics](recurrences-and-asymptotics.qmd)
- [Control and Dynamics](../../../advanced/control-and-dynamics/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

- [MIT 6.1200J graph theory lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-1200j-mathematics-for-computer-science-spring-2024/mit6_1200j_s24_lec11.pdf) - official MIT lecture PDF for graph structure, paths, trees, and related definitions. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-042j-mathematics-for-computer-science-fall-2010/) - official MIT course page for the classic full discrete-math backbone. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS103 graphs lecture I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/archive/cs/cs103/cs103.1252/lectures/05/) - official lecture page for the first graphs pass in a modern discrete-structures course. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 6.1200J graph theory lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-1200j-mathematics-for-computer-science-spring-2024/mit6_1200j_s24_lec11.pdf) - `First pass` - official lecture PDF for graph basics, degree ideas, and trees. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.1200J Mathematics for Computer Science](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-1200j-mathematics-for-computer-science-spring-2024/) - `Second pass` - official course hub connecting graphs with recurrences, asymptotics, and probability. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-042j-mathematics-for-computer-science-fall-2010/) - `Second pass` - classic official MIT course page for the broader discrete-math sequence. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS103 graphs lecture I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/archive/cs/cs103/cs103.1252/lectures/05/) - `First pass` - official lecture page for graph basics and structural interpretation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS103 graphs lecture II](https://web.stanford.edu/class/archive/cs/cs103/cs103.1252/lectures/06/) - `Second pass` - official continuation page for deeper graph and relation structure. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CMU 15-151 course page](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~15151/) - `Second pass` - official course hub for proof-oriented discrete mathematics with graph-theoretic flavor. Checked `2026-04-25`.

Sources checked online on `2026-04-25`:

- MIT 6.1200J lecture 11
- MIT 6.1200J course page
- MIT 6.042J course page
- Stanford CS103 graphs lecture I
- Stanford CS103 graphs lecture II
- CMU 15-151 course page
